questions.question: Where are qubits located in a color code?

options.text: On the edges of the lattice
On the vertices of the lattice

questions.explanation: In color codes, qubits are placed on the vertices of a 3-colorable lattice. This differs from surface codes where qubits are on edges. Stabilizers are associated with faces, with each face having both an X-type and Z-type stabilizer.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of color codes over surface codes?

options.text: Transversal implementation of all Clifford gates

questions.explanation: Color codes allow transversal implementation of all Clifford gates (H, S, CNOT), not just CNOT as in surface codes. This means applying the same gate to each physical qubit implements the logical gate, which is naturally fault-tolerant.

questions.question: Why is a '3-colorable' lattice required for color codes?

options.text: To make the code more colorful
So each face can be assigned one of three colors with no adjacent faces sharing a color
To reduce the number of qubits needed

questions.explanation: A 3-colorable lattice means every face can be colored with one of three colors (R, G, B) such that no two adjacent faces share the same color. This structure enables the transversal Clifford gates and organizes stabilizers by color.

questions.question: The 7-qubit color code is equivalent to which well-known code?
questions.explanation: The 7-qubit color code on a triangular patch is exactly the [[7, 1, 3]] Steane code. Both are CSS codes with the same stabilizer structure, demonstrating how color codes generalize some earlier quantum codes.

questions.question: When a single error occurs in a color code, syndromes appear at:

options.text: Two faces of the same color
All faces containing the affected vertex (all three colors)

questions.explanation: A single error at a vertex triggers syndromes at all faces containing that vertex. In a hexagonal lattice, each vertex belongs to three faces—one of each color (R, G, B). This constrains error locations and aids decoding.

questions.question: What is the approximate circuit-level threshold for color codes?
questions.explanation: Color codes have a circuit-level threshold of approximately 0.1%, which is about 10× lower than surface codes (~1%). This is the trade-off for having transversal Clifford gates.

questions.question: Color codes are self-dual under Hadamard, meaning the transversal Hadamard gate swaps X and Z stabilizers into each other.
questions.explanation: True. Color codes have a self-dual structure where applying transversal Hadamard swaps every X-stabilizer with its corresponding Z-stabilizer on the same face, and vice versa. This is why transversal H works as a logical Hadamard.
